You are a CLEAN-ROOM implementer under the campaign protocol at /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md — read it first and comply strictly.
ACCESS RULES: You must NOT read any file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/crates/, and NOT read any Haskell source (*.hs) of tamarin-prover anywhere on disk (notably /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover/{lib,src}/ and /home/kamilner/tamarin-prover-testing/{lib,src}/). Do NOT read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md (dirty-room document). No web access. You MAY: run the reference binary/servers via your cluster's oracle scripts (export PATH="$PATH:/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin" if invoking directly), read .spthy example files, and read/write freely inside your own cluster directory under /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/. Do not modify anything outside your cluster directory. No git commits.
DISCIPLINE: log every oracle interaction in your workspace QUERIES.log; record derived behavioral facts in BEHAVIOR.md with provenance; append a round section to a REPORT file. Code comments describe current behavior only. Every behavioral claim must trace to a logged probe or captured fixture — never to model-memory of tamarin internals. Return a PLAIN-TEXT summary (no structured output).

UNIT D (console/CLI), round 5 — close the remaining batch-summary content and add a streaming emission contract.
Cluster: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/console/ — read workspace BEHAVIOR.md (§10-§11), QUERIES.log, captures/. Oracle: console/oracle/hs_oracle.sh (use split streams throughout). Crate: workspace/cli-clean.

GAP 1 — summary content your model does not yet cover:
(a) Probe prove-mode runs whose analysis is incomplete or bounded (e.g. --prove with --bound=N on theories whose proofs exceed the bound; partial/failed analyses) and pin any additional summary lines the reference emits in those cases (wording, stream, position) that your render_summary does not produce.
(b) Probe theories that produce BOTH wellformedness warnings AND lemma results: pin the exact separator/joining bytes between the warning block and the lemma lines inside the summary (and between multiple theories' blocks).
Byte-exact captures + tests for each.

GAP 2 — streaming emission (INTEROP REQUIREMENT): the consumer emits output INCREMENTALLY while the prover runs (progress markers and payload lines appear as produced, not after completion). Your framing assembles complete Streams at the end. Provide an incremental API — an emitter/sink the consumer can drive line-by-line (or event-by-event) whose total per-stream byte output is identical to the assembled model (prove equivalence with a test that runs both paths on the same inputs). Design it so the consumer chooses flush timing; you define only content and ordering. All existing tests and fixtures stay green.

Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. - artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". - keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

1	# BEHAVIOR.md — Unit D: CLI/console text surface
2	
3	Every statement below traces to an oracle observation captured under
4	`workspace/captures/` (file names in `[brackets]`) or to the SPEC. No
5	tamarin-prover internals were read; all behavior derived from black-box probing
6	of `oracle/hs_oracle.sh`.
7	
8	Oracle note: `hs_oracle.sh <arg1> [rest...]` consumes `arg1` as the first
9	token then appends the rest, i.e. it runs `tamarin-prover <arg1> <rest...>`
10	with stderr merged into stdout. A true zero-argument invocation is therefore
11	NOT observable through this oracle (it always passes at least one token); the
12	closest observable is a single empty-string token (see Ambiguous mode).
13	
14	## 1. Top-level shape
15	
16	The binary is a multi-mode command (cmdargs-style). The first token selects a
17	**mode** by unique prefix match against the mode names; otherwise the default
18	(batch) mode is used and the token is treated as an input FILE.
19	
20	Modes enumerated from help output `[help_global]`:
21	- (default / no token) = batch mode — positional `FILES`.
22	- `interactive` — "Start a web-server to construct proofs interactively." — positional `WORKDIR`.
23	- `variants` — "Compute the variants of the intruder rules for DH-exponentiation." — no positional.
24	- `test` — "Self-test the tamarin-prover installation." — positional `FILES`.
25	
26	Mode prefix matching `[mode_prefix_int/_v/_t]`: `int`→interactive, `v`→variants,
27	`t`→test (any unambiguous non-empty prefix works). An empty token matches all
28	three → ambiguous `[noargs]`.
29	
30	A flag appearing BEFORE a would-be mode token keeps the default batch mode; the
31	token then becomes a filename `[flag_before_mode]`: `-v interactive` →
32	default mode, "interactive" opened as a file → openFile error.
33	
34	## 2. Exit codes
35	
36	- `--help` / per-mode `--help`, `--version`/`-V`, successful batch run: exit 0.
37	- Every parse error and every runtime error observed: exit 1.
38	
39	## 3. `--help` (global and per-mode)  [help_global, help_interactive, help_variants, help_test]
40	
41	Each mode's help has the same skeleton:
42	
43	    <mode-usage-line>
44	      <mode-description>.
45	
46	    Commands:
47	      interactive  Start a web-server to construct proofs interactively.
48	      variants     Compute the variants of the intruder rules for DH-exponentiation.
49	      test         Self-test the tamarin-prover installation.
50	
51	    Flags:
52	      <flag rows, two-column, right column wrapped to a fixed width>
53	    About:
54	      <help/version rows>
55	
56	    <blank>
57	    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
58	    To show help for different commands, type tamarin-prover [Command] --help.
59	    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
60	    See 'https://github.com/tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover/blob/master/README.md'
61	    for usage instructions and pointers to examples.
62	    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
63	    <blank>
64	
65	Usage lines:
66	- global: `tamarin-prover [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] FILES`
67	- interactive: `interactive [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] WORKDIR`
68	- variants: `variants [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS]`   (no positional)
69	- test: `test [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] FILES`
70	
71	The Commands block is IDENTICAL in all four helps. The footer block (7 lines
72	ending with a trailing blank line) is identical in all four. Every help output
73	ends with `----\n\n` (footer rule + one trailing empty line).
74	
75	The right-hand description column width differs by mode because cmdargs sizes
76	the left column to the widest flag in THAT mode: global uses a very wide left
77	column (widest flag `-m --output-module[=...]`), the other three narrower.
78	Because the exact wrapping/padding is compatibility output, the four help texts
79	are stored verbatim as fixtures and emitted as opaque strings — the clean code
80	does not re-derive column math.
81	
82	Availability of About rows:
83	- default/global mode: `-? --help` AND `-V --version`.
84	- interactive / variants / test: only `-? --help` (no `--version`). Confirmed:
85	  `variants --version` → `Unknown flag: --version` `[version_from_mode]`.
86	
87	### Flag inventory & value semantics (from help text)
88	
89	Value markers: `[=X]` = value OPTIONAL; `=FILE` (no brackets) = value shown
90	required (but see note). Defaults appear in the description text.
91	
92	Default/batch mode flags:
93	- `--prove[=LEMMAPREFIX*|LEMMANAME]`     optional value, repeatable
94	- `--lemma[=LEMMAPREFIX*|LEMMANAME]`     optional value, repeatable
95	- `--stop-on-trace[=DFS|BFS|SEQDFS|SORRY|NONE]`  optional, default DFS
96	- `-b --bound[=INT]`                     optional
97	- `--heuristic[=(C|I|O|P|S|c|i|o|p|s|{.})+]`  optional, default 's'
98	- `--partial-evaluation[=SUMMARY|VERBOSE]`   optional
99	- `-D --defines[=STRING]`                optional
100	- `--diff`                               no value
101	- `--quit-on-warning`                    no value
102	- `--auto-sources`                       no value
103	- `--oraclename[=FILE]`                  optional; default './theory_filename.oracle', fallback './oracle'
104	- `--oracle-only`                        no value
105	- `--quiet`                              no value
106	- `-v --verbose`                         no value
107	- `-c --open-chains[=PositiveInteger]`   optional, default 10
108	- `-s --saturation[=PositiveInteger]`    optional, default 5
109	- `-d --derivcheck-timeout[=INT]`        optional, default 5 (0 deactivates)
110	- `--no-reuse`                           no value
111	- `--no-restrictions`                    no value
112	- `--replication-bound[=INT]`            optional
113	- `--no-compress`                        no value
114	- `--parse-only`                         no value
115	- `--precompute-only`                    no value
116	- `-o --output[=FILE]`                   optional
117	- `-O --Output[=DIR]`                    optional
118	- `-m --output-module[=spthytyped|spthy|msr|proverifequiv|proverif|deepsec]`  optional enum
119	- `--output-json=FILE` (alias `--oj`)    value shown required; NO parse error when omitted [oj_alias_noval,outputjson_noval]
120	- `--output-dot=FILE`  (alias `--od`)    value shown required
121	- `--with-dot[=FILE]`                    optional
122	- `--with-json[=FILE]`                   optional
123	- `--with-maude[=FILE]`                  optional
124	- About: `-? --help`, `-V --version`
125	
126	interactive mode adds: `-p --port[=PORT]`, `-i --interface[=INTERFACE]`,
127	`--image-format[=PNG|SVG]` (default SVG), `--debug`, `--no-logging`; plus the
128	proving/output subset (--prove … --with-maude minus the file-output-module
129	flags: no `--no-compress/--parse-only/--precompute-only/-o/-O/-m/--output-json/
130	--output-dot`). About: only `-? --help`.
131	
132	variants mode: only `-O --Output[=DIR]`; About `-? --help`.
133	
134	test mode: only `--with-dot/--with-json/--with-maude`; About `-? --help`.
135	
136	## 4. `--version` and `-V`  [version, short_version]
137	
138	`--version` and `-V` produce identical output (only valid in default mode).
139	The maude readiness check runs concurrently and its lines INTERLEAVE with the
140	version banner in a fixed, reproducible order:
141	
142	    maude tool: '<MAUDE_PATH>'
143	     checking version: <BANNER line 1: tamarin-prover <VER>, (C) <authors>, <YEARS>>
144	    <blank>
145	    This program comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY. It is free software, and you
146	    are welcome to redistribute it according to its LICENSE, see
147	    'https://github.com/tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover/blob/master/LICENSE'.
148	    <blank>
149	    <MAUDE_VER>. OK.
150	     checking installation: OK.
151	    Generated from:
152	    Tamarin version <VER>
153	    Maude version <MAUDE_VER>
154	    Git revision: <GITREV> (with uncommited changes), branch: <BRANCH>
155	    Compiled at: <TIMESTAMP>
156	
157	The banner text (` checking version: ` prefix + tamarin line + warranty block)
158	is emitted between the maude check's "checking version:" and its "<ver>. OK."
159	because the maude subprocess is being awaited. Dynamic/environment slots:
160	`<MAUDE_PATH>` (default 'maude'), `<VER>` (observed 1.13.0), `<YEARS>`
161	(2010-2023), `<MAUDE_VER>` (3.5.1), `<GITREV>` + dirty-suffix + `<BRANCH>`,
162	`<TIMESTAMP>`. These are build/runtime metadata, injected into the template.
163	
164	## 5. maude preamble (framing prelude)
165	
166	Batch/variants runs that CLOSE a theory (need maude) print a 3-line prelude
167	`[batch_nslpk3_default]`:
168	
169	    maude tool: '<MAUDE_PATH>'
170	     checking version: <MAUDE_VER>. OK.
171	     checking installation: OK.
172	
173	`--parse-only` does NOT print the preamble `[batch_nslpk3_parseonly]` (parsing
174	needs no maude). Runtime file-open errors are printed AFTER this preamble.
175	
176	## 6. Batch-mode framing around a processed theory
177	
178	Full default run `[batch_nslpk3_default]`:
179	
180	    <maude preamble (3 lines)>
181	    [Theory <NAME>] Theory loaded
182	    [Theory <NAME>] Theory translated
183	    [Theory <NAME>] Derivation checks started
184	    [Theory <NAME>] Derivation checks ended
185	    [Theory <NAME>] Theory closed
186	    <OPAQUE THEORY PAYLOAD, from `theory <NAME>` through `end`>
187	    <blank>
188	    ==============================================================================
189	    summary of summaries:
190	    <blank>
191	    analyzed: <ABSOLUTE_FILE_PATH>
192	    <blank>
193	      processing time: <T>s
194	      <-- literal 2-space line
195	    <RESULT LINES>
196	    <blank>
197	    ==============================================================================
198	
199	- `====` rule is exactly 78 `=`. Output ends `====\n` (no trailing content).
200	- `--prove` uses the SAME 5 progress phases `[batch_nslpk3_prove]`.
201	- `--parse-only` prints ONLY `[Theory <NAME>] Theory loaded`, then payload; NO
202	  preamble, NO summary block `[batch_nslpk3_parseonly]`.
203	- The "Generated from:" version comment and any `WARNING:` block are part of the
204	  OPAQUE theory payload (inside `theory … end`), not framing.
205	
206	RESULT LINE forms (2-space indent):
207	- `  <lemma> (all-traces): analysis incomplete (<N> steps)`  [default, unselected]
208	- `  <lemma> (all-traces): verified (<N> steps)`             [proved true]  `[batch_nslpk3_prove]`
209	- `  <lemma> (exists-trace): <result> (<N> steps)`           [exists-trace kind]
210	- (form `falsified (<N> steps)` inferred as the counterpart to `verified`; not
211	  directly captured — see LIMITATIONS.)
212	- `  WARNING: <N> wellformedness check failed!`  [when checks failed; note the
213	  singular "check"] `[round2_multiplication_in_rule_lhs]`
214	
215	When a theory has warnings but no lemmas, the WARNING line is the sole result
216	line. Ordering of the WARNING line vs lemma lines when BOTH are present is not
217	observed (LIMITATIONS).
218	
219	`<T>` (processing time) and `<ABSOLUTE_FILE_PATH>` are dynamic slots.
220	
221	## 7. variants mode run  [mode_variants_run]
222	
223	`variants` (no file): maude preamble, then the pretty-printed intruder
224	variant rules (opaque payload), NO summary block.
225	
226	## 8. Error text taxonomy
227	
228	### 8a. cmdargs parse errors — bare one-liner, exit 1, no help:
229	- Unknown long flag `[err_unknown_flag]`:  `Unknown flag: --foobar`
230	- Unknown short flag `[err_unknown_short]`: `Unknown flag: -Z` (note: `-h` is
231	  NOT help; help is `-?`/`--help`) `[short_help]`
232	- Value given to a valueless flag `[help_eq]`: `Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: --help=foo`
233	- Ambiguous mode `[noargs]`: `Ambiguous mode '', could be any of: interactive variants test`
234	
235	### 8b. cmdargs validation errors — message + blank + FULL help of the active mode, exit 1:
236	- No input file (flags otherwise valid) `[err_stopontrace_bad, err_noinput_diff, err_noinput_prove]`:
237	      error: no input files given
238	      <blank>
239	      <global help body>
240	  Envelope = `error: no input files given\n\n` + exact global help.
241	- Bad interactive WORKDIR `[err_two_modes, interactive_two_pos]`:
242	      error: directory '<DIR>' does not exist.
243	      <blank>
244	      <interactive help body>
245	  (`interactive test` → 'test' is the WORKDIR; `interactive aa bb` → names 'bb'.)
246	
247	### 8c. runtime errors — printed AFTER the maude preamble, exit 1:
248	File open (IOException, no CallStack):
249	- missing `[err_missing_file, token_notfile]`:  `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: does not exist (No such file or directory)`
250	- unreadable `[err_unreadable]`:                 `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: permission denied (Permission denied)`
251	- directory `[err_isdir]`:                       `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: inappropriate type (is a directory)`
252	
253	Application `error` with Haskell CallStack (all cite the same call site):
254	- bad bound `[err_bound_nonint]`:
255	      tamarin-prover: bound: invalid bound given
256	      CallStack (from HasCallStack):
257	        error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch
258	- bad output module `[err_output_module_bad]`:
259	      tamarin-prover: output mode not supported.
260	      CallStack (from HasCallStack):
261	        error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch
262	
263	These runtime strings (incl. the CallStack source path) are compatibility
264	output taken verbatim from the oracle; they are emitted by the runtime layer
265	after parse(), so the clean crate exposes them as render helpers rather than
266	from parse().
267	
268	## 9. LIMITATIONS / unobserved (documented, not guessed in code)
269	- True zero-argument behavior: not observable through this oracle.
270	- `falsified (<N> steps)` exact string: inferred counterpart of `verified`; not
271	  directly captured (no small example produced it within probe budget).
272	- WARNING-line vs lemma-line ordering in the summary when both present.
273	- Whether `--output-json`/`--output-dot` error at output time when value omitted
274	  (only parse-time / --parse-only observed, where no error occurs).
275	- (RESOLVED in R4) Under `--prove`, extra stderr progress lines DO appear after
276	  `Theory closed` — see §10a.
277	- Interactive-mode value validation (`--port`, `--interface`, `--image-format`):
278	  the server starts even on garbage values (`--port=x .` -> exit 124 server run;
279	  `--image-format=BOGUS .` -> server run), so there is NO CLI-level validation
280	  error to reproduce for those flags `[R4 interactive probes]`.
281	
282	## 10. Stream separation (Round 4)  [split_* captures: <label>.out.txt / .err.txt]
283	
284	The Round 1–3 oracle merged streams (`2>&1`). Re-probing with
285	`1>out 2>err` (probes/split_probe.sh) shows a clean, deterministic split. Each
286	stream is byte-complete on its own; the merged-mode interleaving of §4 was purely
287	a flush artifact of combining two FDs.
288	
289	STDOUT carries:
290	- the opaque theory payload(s) `theory … end` `[split_batch_default.out]`,
291	- the whole "summary of summaries" block `[split_batch_default.out]`,
292	- the `--version`/`-V` banner (tamarin line + warranty + `Generated from:` block)
293	  `[split_version.out — banner ONLY; the merged version.tmpl of §4 wrongly bundled
294	  the maude preamble into stdout]`,
295	- every `--help` / per-mode help page `[split_help.out == help_global.txt]`,
296	- the two cmdargs VALIDATION envelopes `error: … \n\n<full help>`
297	  (no-input, bad-workdir) `[split_err_noinput.out == err_noinput_diff.txt,
298	  split_err_workdir.out == err_two_modes.txt]`.
299	
300	STDERR carries:
301	- the 3-line maude preamble (§5) `[split_batch_default.err]`,
302	- the per-theory progress markers `[Theory <name>] <phase>` `[split_batch_default.err]`,
303	- the bare cmdargs one-liners: `Unknown flag`, `Ambiguous mode`,
304	  `Unhandled argument to flag` `[split_err_unknown.err == err_unknown_flag.txt,
305	  split_err_ambig.err == noargs.txt]`,
306	- all runtime errors: file-open failures and the app `error`/CallStack lines
307	  `[split_err_missing.err, split_err_bound.err]`.
308	
309	`--parse-only` STDERR = one `Theory loaded` line per theory, no preamble; STDOUT =
310	concatenated payloads `[split_batch_parseonly.err/out, split_parseonly_multi]`.
311	`variants` STDOUT = variant payload; STDERR = preamble only `[split_variants.*]`.
312	
313	### 10a. Multi-theory framing  [split_batch_multifile.*]
314	- STDERR: preamble once, then each theory's full 5-phase progress block in order.
315	- STDOUT: payloads concatenated directly (payload_k ends `end\n`, payload_{k+1}
316	  begins `theory …` with NO separator), then ONE combined summary listing every
317	  theory. Payloads are printed only for theories NOT written to an output file.
318	
319	Under `--prove`, a theory's stderr progress block gains extra engine lines AFTER
320	`[Theory <name>] Theory closed`, verbatim `[split_batch_prove.err]`:
321	
322	    [Saturating Sources] Step 1 (Max 5)
323	    [Saturating Sources] Done
324	    [Saturating Sources] Step 1 (Max 5)
325	    [Saturating Sources] Done
326	
327	(`Max 5` = the `--saturation` limit.) A plain analyze run emits none of these. The
328	framing model treats these as an opaque per-theory `extra_progress` slot appended
329	to that theory's five closed phases; the engine supplies the exact text.
330	
331	### 10b. summary-of-summaries exact layout  [split_batch_multifile.out, split_batch_output_single.out]
332	The summary section on STDOUT is:
333	
334	    \n                                  <- separates payload from summary (always emitted)
335	    ==============================================================================   (78 '=')
336	    summary of summaries:
337	    <per theory, each preceded by a blank line:>
338	      analyzed: <PATH-as-passed-on-CLI>
339	      <blank>
340	      [  output:          <OUTPUT-PATH>]   <- only when an output file was written
341	        processing time: <T>s
342	      <2-space line "  ">
343	      <result lines, each "  <text>">
344	    <blank>
345	    ==============================================================================
346	    (final newline)
347	
348	Key/value alignment inside the indented block: labels right-padded to the width of
349	`processing time:` (16), then one space, so values start at column 19 →
350	`  output:` + 10 spaces + value; `  processing time: ` + value. `analyzed:` is a
351	separate un-indented header line with a single space. `output:` is the label for
352	BOTH `-o FILE` and `-O DIR` (the latter writes `<inputstem>_analyzed.spthy`).
353	`analyzed:` echoes the path exactly as given on the command line (relative stays
354	relative) `[R4 relative-path probe]`.
355	
356	## 11. Typed value validation (Round 4)
357	
358	Validation fires ONLY when a value string is present (via `=value`, `--oj=…`, or a
359	short-attached `-b5`); a BARE value flag (`--bound`, `--stop-on-trace`, …) never
360	errors `[R4 bare-vs-valued sweep]`. An empty `=` value IS a present value and is
361	validated (`--bound=` → error, `--stop-on-trace=` → `… method: `). All these errors
362	go to STDERR, exit 1, and use the app `error` shape
363	`tamarin-prover: <msg>\nCallStack (from HasCallStack):\n  error, called at
364	src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch`. In a normal run they follow
365	the maude preamble; under `--parse-only` no preamble precedes them.
366	
367	Integer flags → `<LABEL>: invalid bound given` `[R4 numeric sweep]`:
368	`--bound`→`bound`, `-c/--open-chains`→`OpenChainsLimit`,
369	`-s/--saturation`→`SaturationLimit`, `-d/--derivcheck-timeout`→`derivcheck-timeout`,
370	`--replication-bound`→`replication-bound`.
371	Accepted integer set = Haskell `readMaybe Int`: trims leading/trailing ASCII
372	whitespace, optional `-` (with optional whitespace before the digits), decimal /
373	`0x`hex / `0o`octal, silent overflow wrap; REJECTS `+`, `.`, `_`, trailing
374	non-whitespace, and empty. Negatives and 0 are accepted for ALL of them (the
375	"PositiveInteger" help annotation is not enforced at the CLI).
376	
377	Enum flags:
378	- `--stop-on-trace`: value lowercased, valid ∈ {dfs,bfs,seqdfs,sorry,none};
379	  invalid → `unknown stop-on-trace method: <lowercased-value>` (echoes value).
380	- `--partial-evaluation`: case-insensitive, valid ∈ {summary,verbose};
381	  invalid → `partial-evaluation: unknown option` (no echo).
382	- `--output-module` (`-m`): case-SENSITIVE, valid ∈
383	  {spthytyped,spthy,msr,proverifequiv,proverif,deepsec};
384	  invalid → `output mode not supported.` (no echo).
385	`--heuristic` is NOT validated (any chars accepted).
386	
387	Repeated valued flag → LAST occurrence wins / is the one validated
388	`[R4 repeated probe]`.
389	
390	Multi-invalid precedence is a FIXED order, independent of CLI arg order
391	`[R4 precedence sweep, all pairwise relations consistent]`:
392	`stop-on-trace > bound > partial-evaluation > open-chains > saturation >
393	output-module > derivcheck-timeout > replication-bound`.
394	
395	### 11a. Consumer-extension flags (NOT in the reference, NOT in help)
396	`--processors <positive int>`, `--maude-processes <positive int>`,
397	`--data-dir <path>` all yield `Unknown flag: --<name>` in the reference
398	`[R4 interop probe]`, confirming they are absent. The clean crate accepts them as
399	typed flags with its OWN validation (positive integer; any path); their rejection
400	texts are original (no reference to reproduce) and they are omitted from help.
401

1	//! Output-stream model.
2	//!
3	//! Every byte the binary emits is destined for exactly one of the two standard
4	//! streams. Which stream a given piece of text lands on is a fixed, probed
5	//! property (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §10): the theory payload, the summary
6	//! block, help pages, the version banner, and the `error: … + full help`
7	//! validation envelopes go to stdout; the maude preamble, per-theory progress
8	//! markers, the bare cmdargs one-liners, and all runtime errors go to stderr.
9	
10	/// One of the two standard output streams.
11	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
12	pub enum Stream {
13	    /// Standard output.
14	    Out,
15	    /// Standard error.
16	    Err,
17	}
18	
19	/// A pair of independently byte-exact stream buffers.
20	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, PartialEq, Eq)]
21	pub struct Streams {
22	    pub out: String,
23	    pub err: String,
24	}
25	
26	impl Streams {
27	    /// Append `text` to the buffer for `stream`.
28	    pub fn push(&mut self, stream: Stream, text: &str) {
29	        match stream {
30	            Stream::Out => self.out.push_str(text),
31	            Stream::Err => self.err.push_str(text),
32	        }
33	    }
34	}

1	//! Version banner and the maude readiness preamble.
2	//!
3	//! `--version`/`-V` writes to two streams (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §10): the
4	//! banner (tamarin line + warranty block + `Generated from:` block) goes to
5	//! stdout, and the maude readiness preamble goes to stderr. They only appeared
6	//! interleaved under the merged oracle. The stdout banner is stored as a template
7	//! with `{{SLOT}}` markers for the build/runtime metadata; the static text is
8	//! byte-exact observed output.
9	
10	use crate::stream::Streams;
11	
12	const VERSION_TEMPLATE: &str = include_str!("../fixtures/version.tmpl");
13	
14	/// Maude tool identity used in the readiness preamble.
15	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
16	pub struct MaudeInfo {
17	    /// Tool path as printed inside the quotes (default `maude`).
18	    pub path: String,
19	    /// Reported maude version, e.g. `3.5.1`.
20	    pub version: String,
21	}
22	
23	impl Default for MaudeInfo {
24	    fn default() -> Self {
25	        MaudeInfo { path: "maude".to_string(), version: "3.5.1".to_string() }
26	    }
27	}
28	
29	/// Build/runtime metadata that fills the version banner slots.
30	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
31	pub struct VersionInfo {
32	    pub maude: MaudeInfo,
33	    pub tamarin_version: String,
34	    pub copyright_years: String,
35	    /// Everything after `Git revision: ` (revision + dirty note + branch).
36	    pub git_description: String,
37	    /// The `Compiled at:` timestamp text.
38	    pub compiled_at: String,
39	}
40	
41	/// The stdout version banner, filling every slot in the observed template.
42	pub fn render_version(info: &VersionInfo) -> String {
43	    VERSION_TEMPLATE
44	        .replace("{{TAMARIN_VERSION}}", &info.tamarin_version)
45	        .replace("{{COPYRIGHT_YEARS}}", &info.copyright_years)
46	        .replace("{{MAUDE_VERSION}}", &info.maude.version)
47	        .replace("{{GIT_DESCRIPTION}}", &info.git_description)
48	        .replace("{{COMPILED_AT}}", &info.compiled_at)
49	}
50	
51	/// The full `--version`/`-V` output across both streams: the banner on stdout and
52	/// the maude readiness preamble on stderr.
53	pub fn frame_version(info: &VersionInfo) -> Streams {
54	    Streams { out: render_version(info), err: maude_preamble(&info.maude) }
55	}
56	
57	/// The 3-line maude readiness preamble emitted before batch/variants processing
58	/// (each line terminated by a newline).
59	pub fn maude_preamble(maude: &MaudeInfo) -> String {
60	    format!(
61	        "maude tool: '{}'\n checking version: {}. OK.\n checking installation: OK.\n",
62	        maude.path, maude.version
63	    )
64	}

1	theory NSLPK3
2	begin
3	
4	builtins: asymmetric-encryption
5	
6	/*
7	   Protocol:    The classic three message version of the
8	                Needham-Schroeder-Lowe Public Key Protocol
9	   Modeler:     Simon Meier
10	   Date:        June 2012
11	   Source:      Modeled after the description by Paulson in
12	                Isabelle/HOL/Auth/NS_Public.thy.
13	
14	   Status:      working
15	
16	   Note that we are using explicit global constants for discerning the
17	   different encryption instead of the implicit sources.
18	 */
19	
20	
21	// Public key infrastructure
22	rule Register_pk:
23	  [ Fr(~ltkA) ]
24	  -->
25	  [ !Ltk($A, ~ltkA), !Pk($A, pk(~ltkA)), Out(pk(~ltkA)) ]
26	
27	rule Reveal_ltk:
28	  [ !Ltk(A, ltkA) ] --[ RevLtk(A)    ]-> [ Out(ltkA) ]
29	
30	
31	/* We formalize the following protocol
32	
33	  protocol NSLPK3 {
34	    1. I -> R: {'1',ni,I}pk(R)
35	    2. I <- R: {'2',ni,nr,R}pk(I)
36	    3. I -> R: {'3',nr}pk(R)
37	  }
38	*/
39	
40	rule I_1:
41	  let m1 = aenc{'1', ~ni, $I}pkR
42	  in
43	    [ Fr(~ni)
44	    , !Pk($R, pkR)
45	    ]
46	  --[ OUT_I_1(m1)
47	    ]->
48	    [ Out( m1 )
49	    , St_I_1($I, $R, ~ni)
50	    ]
51	
52	rule R_1:
53	  let m1 = aenc{'1', ni, I}pk(ltkR)
54	      m2 = aenc{'2', ni, ~nr, $R}pkI
55	  in
56	    [ !Ltk($R, ltkR)
57	    , In( m1 )
58	    , !Pk(I, pkI)
59	    , Fr(~nr)
60	    ]
61	  --[ IN_R_1_ni( ni, m1 )
62	    , OUT_R_1( m2 )
63	    , Running(I, $R, <'init',ni,~nr>)
64	    ]->
65	    [ Out( m2 )
66	    , St_R_1($R, I, ni, ~nr)
67	    ]
68	
69	rule I_2:
70	  let m2 = aenc{'2', ni, nr, R}pk(ltkI)
71	      m3 = aenc{'3', nr}pkR
72	  in
73	    [ St_I_1(I, R, ni)
74	    , !Ltk(I, ltkI)
75	    , In( m2 )
76	    , !Pk(R, pkR)
77	    ]
78	  --[ IN_I_2_nr( nr, m2)
79	    , Commit (I, R, <'init',ni,nr>)  // need to log identities explicitely to
80	    , Running(R, I, <'resp',ni,nr>)  // specify that they must not be
81	                                     // compromised in the property.
82	    ]->
83	    [ Out( m3 )
84	    , Secret(I,R,nr)
85	    , Secret(I,R,ni)
86	    ]
87	
88	rule R_2:
89	    [ St_R_1(R, I, ni, nr)
90	    , !Ltk(R, ltkR)
91	    , In( aenc{'3', nr}pk(ltkR) )
92	    ]
93	  --[ Commit (R, I, <'resp',ni,nr>)
94	    ]->
95	    [ Secret(R,I,nr)
96	    , Secret(R,I,ni)
97	    ]
98	
99	/* TODO: Also model session-key reveals and adapt security properties. */
100	rule Secrecy_claim:
101	  [ Secret(A, B, m) ] --[ Secret(A, B, m) ]-> []
102	
103	
104	
105	/* Note that we are using an untyped protocol model.
106	The contents of the 'ni' variable in rule R_1 may therefore in general be any
107	message. This leads to unsolved chain constraints when checking what message
108	can be extracted from the message sent by rule R_1. In order to get rid of
109	these constraints, we require the following sources invariant that relates the
110	point of instantiation to the point of sending by either the adversary or the
111	initiator.
112	
113	In order to understand the use of this sources invariant you might try the
114	following experiment. Comment out this sources invariant and then check the
115	precomputed case distinctions in the GUI. Try to complete the proof of the
116	'nonce_secrecy' lemma.
117	*/
118	lemma types [sources]:
119	  " (All ni m1 #i.
120	       IN_R_1_ni( ni, m1) @ i
121	       ==>
122	       ( (Ex #j. KU(ni) @ j & j < i)
123	       | (Ex #j. OUT_I_1( m1 ) @ j)
124	       )
125	    )
126	  & (All nr m2 #i.
127	       IN_I_2_nr( nr, m2) @ i
128	       ==>
129	       ( (Ex #j. KU(nr) @ j & j < i)
130	       | (Ex #j. OUT_R_1( m2 ) @ j)
131	       )
132	    )
133	  "
134	
135	// Nonce secrecy from the perspective of both the initiator and the responder.
136	lemma nonce_secrecy:
137	  " /* It cannot be that */
138	    not(
139	        Ex A B s #i.
140	          /* somebody claims to have setup a shared secret, */
141	          Secret(A, B, s) @ i
142	          /* but the adversary knows it */
143	        & (Ex #j. K(s) @ j)
144	          /* without having performed a long-term key reveal. */
145	        & not (Ex #r. RevLtk(A) @ r)
146	        & not (Ex #r. RevLtk(B) @ r)
147	       )"
148	
149	// Injective agreement from the perspective of both the initiator and the responder.
150	lemma injective_agree:
151	  " /* Whenever somebody commits to running a session, then*/
152	    All actor peer params #i.
153	        Commit(actor, peer, params) @ i
154	      ==>
155	        /* there is somebody running a session with the same parameters */
156	          (Ex #j. Running(actor, peer, params) @ j & j < i
157	            /* and there is no other commit on the same parameters */
158	            & not(Ex actor2 peer2 #i2.
159	                    Commit(actor2, peer2, params) @ i2 & not(#i = #i2)
160	                 )
161	          )
162	        /* or the adversary perform a long-term key reveal on actor or peer */
163	        | (Ex #r. RevLtk(actor) @ r)
164	        | (Ex #r. RevLtk(peer)  @ r)
165	  "
166	
167	// Consistency check: ensure that secrets can be shared between honest agents.
168	lemma session_key_setup_possible:
169	  exists-trace
170	  " /* It is possible that */
171	    Ex A B s #i.
172	      /* somebody claims to have setup a shared secret, */
173	      Secret(A, B, s) @ i
174	      /* without the adversary having performed a long-term key reveal. */
175	    & not (Ex #r. RevLtk(A) @ r)
176	    & not (Ex #r. RevLtk(B) @ r)
177	  "
178	
179	end
180

5	theory FreshPubConst
6	begin
7	
8	rule Bad:
9	   [ ]
10	  --[ ]->
11	   [ Out(~'foo') ]
12	
13	end

## 12. Summary body content (Round 5)  [r5_* captures]

The `<result lines>` slot of §6/§10b (the summary-of-summaries body under the
`processing time:` line) has more structure than §6 recorded. Probed with
self-authored theories `probes/round5/*.spthy` (own fixtures, not the GPL
examples), split-stream, on both `--prove` and default runs.

### 12a. Body layout — an opening line plus up to two sections
After the `  processing time: <T>s` line the body is, byte-exactly:

    "  \n"                                   <- opening two-space line (ALWAYS present)
    <warning section, if any>                <- comes first
    "  \n" between the two sections          <- present iff BOTH sections present
    <lemma section, if any>

i.e. an opening `  ` line, then the present sections joined by another `  ` line.
- No warnings, no lemmas → body is just `  \n` `[r5_nolemma_default]`.
- Lemmas only → `  \n` + lemma lines `[split_batch_default / r5_exists_verified]`.
- Warning only → `  \n` + warning section `[round2_fresh_public_constant / r5_freshpub_prove]`.
- Warning + lemmas → `  \n` + warning + `  \n` + lemmas `[r5_warn_lemma_*]`.

### 12b. Warning section — one or two lines
    "  WARNING: <N> wellformedness check failed!\n"   (noun stays singular "check")
    "           The analysis results might be wrong!\n"   (SECOND line, conditional)
The advisory second line is left-padded 11 spaces — exactly the width of the
prefix `  WARNING: ` — so it aligns under the text after `WARNING: `. It is
emitted **iff the run is a proving run (`--prove`)**, independent of whether any
lemma was actually proved: a `--prove` run whose lemma prefix matches nothing
(all lemmas "analysis incomplete") still emits it `[r5_falsify_nomatch]`, and a
proving run with a warning but no lemmas emits it with no following separator
`[r5_freshpub_prove]`; a plain analyze run never does `[r5_warn_lemma_default]`.
A run with no warning never shows this line regardless of `--prove`
`[r5_nslpk3_prove_bound2, r5_nolemma_prove]`.

### 12c. Lemma verdict lines — `  <name> (<kind>): <verdict> (<N> steps)`
Observed `<verdict>` forms (the step count is the explored proof depth):
- `verified` — uniform for BOTH `all-traces` and `exists-trace`
  `[r5_exists_verified: can_ping/always_true both "verified"]`.
- `analysis incomplete` — default/unselected lemmas, AND `--prove` lemmas cut off
  by `--bound` (only the step count grows; NO extra line for the bound)
  `[r5_nslpk3_prove_bound2: nonce_secrecy "analysis incomplete (6 steps)"]`.
- `falsified` — carries a **kind-dependent suffix** `[r5_falsify_prove]`:
  - all-traces → `falsified - found trace` (a counter-example trace was produced),
  - exists-trace → `falsified - no trace found` (no witnessing trace exists).
  (Corrects the R2 guess that falsified prints as a bare `falsified`.)

### 12d. Between-theory joining (multi-file)  [r5_multi_warn_prove]
Each theory's block is preceded by a single `\n` (blank line) before its
`analyzed:` header — unchanged from §10b; confirmed with a lemmas-only theory
followed by a warning+lemma theory. The per-theory warning/lemma structure of
§12a–c is entirely local to each block.

Corresponding STDERR (unchanged framing, §10a): preamble once, then per theory
its five closed phases + any `[Saturating Sources] …` extra progress; the number
of saturating-sources lines is theory-specific (NSLPK3 emits four, the tiny
`WarnAndLemma`/`FreshPubConst` theories emit none) — treated as the opaque
per-theory `extra_progress` slot.

## 13. Streaming emission contract (Round 5, INTEROP)

The assembled framing of §6/§10 hands back complete `Streams` at the end. A
consumer that flushes output *as the prover runs* needs to drive emission
event-by-event. The clean crate adds an incremental surface (`src/emit.rs`) that
is a pure re-ordering of the SAME byte content — no new observable strings, so no
new oracle facts; it is an internal API shape, verified by equivalence against
the assembled model rather than against the oracle:
- `Sink::emit(stream, text)` — the consumer's byte destination; the consumer
  chooses flush timing (flush-per-call = true line streaming; buffering = coalesced).
- `BatchEmitter` — `begin` (preamble→stderr) / `progress`,`closed_phases`,
  `extra_progress` (→stderr) / `payload` (→stdout) / `record_summary` (buffered) /
  `finish` (combined summary→stdout). The summary is the one unavoidable trailing
  barrier (it spans every theory), matching the reference which prints it last.
- Equivalence property: for identical inputs the emitter's per-stream bytes equal
  `frame_batch`'s. The cross-stream interleaving differs in wall-clock time (the
  point of streaming) but each stream's byte sequence is identical, because on
  each stream both paths emit in the same order: stderr = preamble then per-theory
  progress; stdout = payloads in order then the single summary. Proven by tests
  `streaming_matches_assembled_on_shared_inputs` and
  `streaming_reproduces_captured_multifile_streams`.

3. **Bounded/incomplete proofs add no new line.** A `--prove` run cut off by
   `--bound` still prints `analysis incomplete (<explored> steps)`; only the step
   count changes. `[r5_nslpk3_prove_bound2]`
4. **Warning + lemmas in one theory** (GAP 1b): the warning section precedes the
   lemma lines, separated by a `  ` (two-space) line. Under `--prove` the warning

   `--prove` prefix matches zero lemmas). `[r5_warn_lemma_prove/_default,
   r5_freshpub_prove, r5_falsify_nomatch]`

   (warning, then lemmas) joined by a `  ` line; empty when there is neither.
   `[r5_nolemma_default]`

   `analyzed:` header, confirmed with a lemmas-only theory followed by a
   warning+lemma theory. `[r5_multi_warn_prove]`
